name: sh (Pythonic Subprocess Interface)

performance.notes: Subprocess fork overhead; similar to subprocess module

agent_readiness.notes: Run any shell command as a Python function. import sh. sh.ls('-la'). sh.git.status(). sh.python('-c', 'print(1)'). Raises ErrorReturnCode on failure. Pipes: sh.grep(sh.ls('-la'), 'pyc'). Env vars: sh.env(PATH='/usr/bin'). Background: proc = sh.long_running(_bg=True). proc.wait(). Streaming: for line in sh.tail('-f', 'log.txt', _iter=True): print(line). SSH: sh.ssh('user@host', 'uptime'). _in for stdin, _out/_err for capture. _timeout for time limits. macOS/Linux only (not Windows). v2 is a rewrite — different from v1.

gotchas: Linux/macOS only — does NOT work on Windows (use subprocess)
sh v2 is a rewrite with breaking changes from v1
Shell injection risk if passing user input directly — always use argument list form
Complex piping can be counterintuitive vs bash pipe syntax
Some commands behave differently under sh (interactive vs non-interactive)
best_when: Scripting tools needing clean Python subprocess invocation
Replacing bash scripts with Python while keeping command syntax
Building deployment scripts that call git, docker, kubectl, etc.
summary: sh lets you call any shell command as a Python function. import sh; sh.ls('-la'); sh.git.status(); sh.grep(sh.ls(), '.py'). Streaming: for line in sh.tail('-f', 'log', _iter=True). Background: proc = sh.cmd(_bg=True). MIT. Linux/macOS only (not Windows). v2 is a rewrite. Shell injection risk with user input — use arg list form. Best for scripting tools replacing bash. Use invoke for task runners.
